name: Ek Chuaj
description: Ek Chuaj, also known as Ek Chuah, is a significant deity in the pantheon of the pre-Columbian Maya civilization, primarily associated with merchants, cacao, and warfare. He is often depicted with a distinctive appearance, characterized by a protruding lower lip, a long, pendulous nose, and black markings, which often signified his association with trade routes and the goods transported along them. Ek Chuaj's influence extended beyond mere commerce; he was also invoked for successful journeys and protection from dangers encountered during travel, particularly those related to the exchange of valuable commodities. He was a patron of cacao farmers, a highly prized commodity used in rituals and as currency. While primarily associated with prosperity and commerce, Ek Chuaj also held a martial aspect, suggesting the defense of trade routes and the acquisition of goods through conquest, blurring the lines between economic activity and the power dynamics of the time. Representations of Ek Chuaj are found across various Mayan sites, underscoring his widespread importance and the complex interplay of trade, warfare, and ritual within Mayan society.
subtitle: Merchant Protector

keyMyths.title: Ek Chuaj and the Lost Caravan
keyMyths.description: A myth recounts how a large merchant caravan, laden with valuable goods, became lost deep in the jungle. Facing starvation and attack by wild animals, the merchants prayed to Ek Chuaj. The deity, moved by their plight, appeared as a jaguar, guiding them through treacherous paths and leading them to safety and a bountiful village where they could resupply. This established the tradition of merchants offering prayers and small offerings to Ek Chuaj before embarking on long journeys.

keyMyths.title: The Gift of Cacao
keyMyths.description: Legend states that Ek Chuaj discovered the secret of cultivating cacao, a plant previously unknown to humankind. He traveled to the underworld, Xibalba, and through cunning and bravery, he managed to steal the cacao seeds from the gods of death. Returning to the mortal realm, he taught the Maya people how to cultivate the plant, leading to prosperity and the development of complex rituals centered around the consumption of cacao.

keyMyths.title: Ek Chuaj's Defense of the Trade City
keyMyths.description: During a period of intense conflict between Mayan city-states, a wealthy trade city found itself under siege. The desperate citizens prayed to Ek Chuaj for protection. The deity manifested as a powerful warrior, leading the city's defenders in a fierce battle. He inspired them with courage and tactical knowledge, enabling them to repel the invaders and secure their city's trade routes, solidifying Ek Chuaj's role as a protector of commerce through military strength.

sources.author: Michael D. Coe
